source: Furness, New Variorum Hamlet (1877) — line-keyed notes. Demo excerpt.

notes.lemma: How now! a rat? Dead, for a ducat, dead!
notes.note: Furness gathers the debate on Hamlet's knowledge behind the arras. Does he take the hidden listener for the King? His own next line — 'Is it the king?' — is collected as evidence that he does not know, though editors note it can be played as horror rather than genuine ignorance. Central to any trial of the killing.

notes.lemma: Nay, I know not: Is it the king?
notes.note: The single strongest textual surface for the question of intent. Furness: the line 'seems decisive that Hamlet did not know whom he had killed'; contrary staging tradition has Hamlet hope it is the King. The ambiguity is why the closet killing resists a settled legal reading.

notes.lemma: I took thee for thy better
notes.note: Read alongside 3.4.31: 'thy better' is glossed as the King. Furness treats it as confirmation that Hamlet aimed at Claudius, not Polonius — manslaughter of Polonius in the attempt on the King, or transferred intent. The line the prosecution must confront and the defense must lean on.
